title: Everplay shares slide 13% despite record profits and upbeat outlook as flat revenues spook investors
content:
Everplay (AIM:EVPL), the independent video games developer and publisher, saw its shares fall 13% to 225p on Tuesday despite reporting record profits and an upbeat outlook for 2026, in a move analysts suggested was an overreaction to headline revenue figures that had already been well-flagged.

Peel Hunt, which rates the stock a 'buy' with a target price of 480p, noted that flat revenues were anticipated, reflecting the company's deliberate decision to exit the low-margin physical distribution business operated through its astragon division.

The broker also pointed out that the shares trade on just 10 times its 2026 earnings estimate, and upgraded its 2026 EBITDA forecast by 3% following the results.

Stripping out the physical distribution exit, group revenue grew 5% to £166.0 million (2024: £166.6 million).

Peel noted that while revenue fell 10% in the first half of 2025, it accelerated 9% in the second half, suggesting underlying momentum was building through the year.

Gross profit rose 10% to £76.3 million, and the gross profit margin expanded by 4.4 percentage points to 46.0%, illustrating the benefit of shedding lower-quality revenues. Peel Hunt attributed the improvement to the absence of material title impairments, a favourable sales mix, and continued cost discipline.

Adjusted EBITDA grew 11% to £48.5 million, bang in line with Peel Hunt's estimates, while profit before tax jumped 44% to £36.6 million, with adjusted profit before tax coming in slightly ahead of the broker's forecasts.

The group ended the year with cash of £51.9 million, below Peel Hunt's £63 million estimate, a shortfall the broker attributed to working capital timing, higher tax rates, which it said could reverse, and capital expenditure on upcoming first-party titles.

The broker said the cash position nonetheless left room for further acquisitions.

New release revenues were a particular bright spot, rising 80% year on year, driven by six new games from the Team17 label, including Date Everything!, which attracted more than 750,000 players since launch.

StoryToys, the group's children's edutainment division, delivered an outstanding year with revenues up 25% to £30.4 million, boosted by the LEGO Bluey app, which passed one million downloads in its first month and reached number one in the kids iPad chart in 117 countries.

The group enters 2026 with a pipeline of at least 15 new games and apps, including Hell Let Loose: Vietnam, which has already accumulated more than half a million wishlist entries on gaming platform Steam, along with new titles arising from partnerships with Netflix, Apple Arcade, Amazon and Nintendo Switch 2.

Chief executive Mikkel Weider, who joined in January, said he was confident the group was on track for a strong year, with the board declaring a final dividend of 1.9 pence per share, bringing the full-year total to 2.9 pence, up 7% on 2024 and a 4% beat to Peel's estimate.

title: Netflix Shares Upgraded to Buy As Revenue Set To Rise
content:
Netflix shares (NASDAQ:NFLX) have climbed more than 19% over the past month following the streaming giant’s decision to walk away from a bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery. A fresh analyst upgrade today that highlights accelerating revenue growth that outpaces the broader media sector has also added a boost to NFLX bulls.

The Netflix stock price has rallied sharply through late February, after a difficult start to 2026, with the gains coming on the heels of Netflix’s withdrawal from the Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition process. The move, which saw Netflix decline to match a competing $31-per-share cash offer from Paramount/Skydance, was met with relief by markets concerned about potential balance sheet strain and execution risk from a transformational merger.

The positive momentum received additional support today as Erste Group upgraded Netflix to Buy from Hold, citing the company’s superior growth trajectory relative to legacy media competitors and digital entertainment peers.

Smarter investing starts with better information. The Bull Premium gives you everything you need to grow your wealth and build your future. You will get:

Sign up today and get your first week free

According to the Erste analyst report, Netflix is increasing both revenue and earnings per share “much faster than its competitors,” with expectations that the company will reach approximately $52 billion in revenue by 2026. That figure represents roughly 14% year-over-year growth from anticipated 2025 levels and would mark a meaningful acceleration from the mid-single-digit expansion rates the company posted during its 2022-2023 slowdown period.

The projection aligns with Netflix’s own guidance trajectory. The company generated approximately $39 billion in revenue during 2024 and has guided to a range of $43.5 billion to $44.5 billion for 2025, implying 11% to 13% growth. Management has discussed an internal aspiration to double revenue to roughly $80 billion by 2030, supported by continued subscriber additions, pricing optimization, and a sharp ramp in advertising revenue.

Recent quarterly results underscore the growth reacceleration. Netflix reported fourth-quarter 2025 revenue of approximately $12 billion, up 17% to 18% year-over-year, while operating margins reached 24% to 25%, slightly ahead of guidance. Paid memberships have surpassed 325 million globally, with net additions continuing to benefit from the company’s password-sharing crackdown and the rollout of advertising-supported subscription tiers.

The decision to exit the Warner Bros. Discovery bidding process represents a significant strategic pivot. Netflix had pursued the complex transaction with the aim of acquiring Warner Bros. studios, HBO and HBO Max streaming assets, DC Entertainment franchises, and related intellectual property. However, when Paramount/Skydance raised its competing bid and structured terms that WBD’s board deemed superior, Netflix management opted not to escalate.

Market commentary around the withdrawal emphasized that Netflix walked away with approximately $2.8 billion in termination compensation under the existing merger agreement, while avoiding the leverage, integration complexity, and regulatory scrutiny that would have accompanied a deal of that magnitude. The company had paused share repurchases to accumulate cash for the potential acquisition, having bought back roughly $2.1 billion of stock in the fourth quarter against an $8 billion authorization. With the transaction off the table, markets now anticipate a resumption and potential expansion of the buyback program.

A critical element of the bull case centers on Netflix’s advertising business, which has emerged as a high-growth revenue stream. The ad-supported tier generated approximately $1.5 billion in revenue during 2025, representing growth of roughly 250% year-over-year. Management has indicated expectations for ad revenue to double again in 2026 as the company expands programmatic access, introduces new ad formats, and scales the offering across additional markets. Internal targets discussed in investor forums point to advertising revenue reaching approximately $9 billion by 2030, forming a substantial portion of the company’s plan to double total revenue over the decade.

Margin performance has also strengthened materially. Operating leverage on technology, marketing, and general administrative expenses, combined with content spending that is growing more slowly than revenue, has allowed Netflix to expand profitability even as it invests in live sports, gaming, and international content. Full-year 2024 net income reached approximately $8.7 billion, a step-change improvement from pre-2022 levels, while management has guided to roughly $8 billion in free cash flow for 2025.

The combination of revenue reacceleration and margin expansion has prompted analysts to reassess Netflix’s valuation multiple. The stock currently trades at approximately 26 times forward earnings, a level that sits near the company’s own historical range during the 2022-2023 period when growth was materially slower. Bulls argue that a mid-20s price-to-earnings ratio is reasonable, if not attractive, for a business generating mid-teens revenue growth, expanding margins, and producing substantial free cash flow with optionality in advertising, live content, and gaming.

The competitive landscape has also evolved in Netflix’s favor. Traditional media companies continue to struggle with linear television declines, weaker balance sheets, and streaming operations that remain unprofitable or marginally profitable. Pure-play digital competitors often trade at higher multiples despite slower growth or weaker unit economics. Netflix’s scale, global footprint, and operational execution have solidified its position as the clear leader in subscription streaming, with the largest paid membership base and the most robust profitability profile in the sector.

Despite the turnaround, some bears remain firm in their stance. Subscriber growth in mature markets such as the United States, Canada, and parts of Western Europe is slowing, meaning incremental expansion must increasingly come from emerging markets where average revenue per user is lower, or from price increases that carry churn risk. The company’s recent growth has leaned heavily on pricing optimization and the advertising ramp, creating dependence on execution in areas where Netflix has less historical track record.

Competitive dynamics remain fluid. Rivals including Disney, Amazon, Apple, and a potentially restructured Warner Bros. Discovery under new ownership will continue to invest heavily in content and technology. The risk exists that competitors narrow the engagement gap or secure exclusive franchises that constrain Netflix’s pricing power or force higher content spending to maintain its leadership position.

Additionally, the withdrawal from the Warner Bros. Discovery transaction, while praised for capital discipline, means Netflix forgoes potential strategic assets including HBO’s premium brand, Warner Bros.’ film library and production capabilities, and DC’s superhero franchises. Some analysts view the decision as a missed opportunity to cement an unassailable content advantage and unlock cost and revenue synergies through bundling, content windowing, and intellectual property monetization across gaming and consumer products.

Today’s upgrade and the positive market reaction to the Warner Bros. Discovery withdrawal reflect growing confidence that management can deliver on that formula without taking undue balance sheet or execution risk. Yet the path to $52 billion in 2026 revenue and beyond requires flawless execution across multiple initiatives in an increasingly competitive and maturing market, leaving the stock vulnerable to any signs that growth is decelerating faster than anticipated or that profitability gains are plateauing.

title: Millions of tenants to get letters from landlords about new rights | Money blog
content:
Read about renters getting stronger rights, pet owners facing cheaper bills and our latest Money Problem below.

Tuesday 24 March 2026 19:18, UK

We're signing off for the day now - and we'll leave you with some evening reading.

Last year we began product testing everyday items and if you want to catch up with the series, you can do so here - and let us know in the comments box which products you'd like to see tested.

The Competition and Markets Authority's announcement of a price cap on pet prescriptions has elicited an emotional response from Money readers.

"About time on the pet prescriptions. Good news. Outrageous abuses and profiteering were unchecked."

"Yay, finally. With four cats and three dogs, this is a blessing."

"That's brilliant. Was just about to get a check-up for dogs. And when you need an ultrasound, the price is ridiculous."

Others fear that vets could be amping up other charges in response:

"They will simply raise medicine or treatment costs."

This reader says the new measures are not going far enough:

"What cost to the taxpayers was the three-year CMA 'investigation'? The result was pathetic. These vets run what is essentially a cartel. The prices they charge bear no relationship to the cost. Among friends and family, we come across frequent stories of price gouging."

Quite a few of you have also shared your own experiences with what many consider exaggerated prices:

"We paid £36 for 10ml eye drops at our vets then we learned we could have got them for £12 online."

"We are with a CVS practice. They charged us almost £90 for medication that we can buy off the direct website for £27 including postage."

"My dog became sick a month ago. I was charged £587 for blood tests and £120 for 10 days of antibiotics biotics. No prewarning. We were told: 'Oh, we could consider putting her down!' I subsequently sourced the exact antibiotic from Europe for 14 euros! Absolute disgrace, UK...

The median pay rise for the start of the year was 3.3%, slightly above inflation, new data shows.

By the end of the year, that average will fall to 3%, in line with 2025, according to predictions by HR consultants Brightmine.

"What we're seeing is employers becoming more deliberate in how they set pay," says Sheila Attwood, senior content manager at Brightmine.

Staff across the country could face anything from a pay freeze to a 10% hike, but only 7.7% of pay awards are expected to surpass the 5% threshold, according to Brightmine.

"Rather than reacting to short-term movements, many are anchoring decisions to what they believe is sustainable over the year ahead, particularly given ongoing cost pressures and an easing labour market," says Attwood.

"That more measured approach is translating into tight pay parameters."

It's not all bad news, though - some 93.7% of employers still intend to award pay rises rather than implement freezes, Brightmine's survey shows.

The UK government is introducing rules to crack down on late payments to small businesses, including freelancers, family firms and the self-employed.

Under the new rules, payment terms must not allow more than 60 days for large firms to pay small businesses.

New mandatory interest of 8% above the Bank of England rate will be introduced for all late payments.

A small business that is owed £10,000 by one of its customers would then receive £10,293.15 if it's paid too late.

The government says the new rules will "ease the cost of living for entrepreneurs and SME owners", noting some 38 businesses shut their doors every single day because they are not paid on time.

Millions of tenants across England will receive a letter from their landlord explaining their new rights.

Around 11 million renters will benefit from stronger protections under the Renters' Rights Act from the start of May.

This includes the scrapping of Section 21 evictions, better rights to request pets, protections against rent increases and fixed-term tenancies.

"The Renters' Rights Act is a major step towards re-balancing power between renters and landlords, giving us greater security in our homes," Ben Twomey, chief executive of Generation Rent, said.

"Ahead of the new law coming into action, it’s vital renters take the time to understand what these new rights are and how to respond if landlords break the rules."

You could receive a hard copy of the information in the post, or it could be attached as a PDF to an email.

Landlords and their letting agents are legally required to provide it to you by 31 May, or they could face a fine.

According to the government website, the document is designed to give you "helpful and practical" advice on each of the changes and what it means for your tenancy agreement.

A version has been published online by the government here.

We learnt this morning that changes will be made to the vet sector this year, including a £21 cap on prescriptions and a requirement for practices to publish "comprehensive" price lists.

In its report, the Competition and Markets Authority said "specific" issues had been found at six large vet practices, so we asked them for their reaction to the reforms...

Vets for Pets, which is part of Pets at Home, said it had been a "challenging time" for its teams, but it was pleased to hear the system would be modernised.

A spokesperson said it was the only large group whose average prices were not found to be higher than independent practices.

"Our network of over 600 practice owners are dedicated to providing the best quality and value pet care in the communities they serve," they added.

"This has been a challenging time for dedicated veterinary teams who work tirelessly to provide care to the nation's pets, but we're pleased the process has identified the need to modernise the legislation and regulatory system underpinning the sector."

CVS, which has hundreds of practices, said in a statement to investors that it thought some of the CMA reforms were not "fully justified", but that it was "comfortable with them and believe they are workable".

"We already comply with many remedies and are well advanced in complying with others, with price lists published on our practice websites in late 2025 and 117 CVS UK companion animal practices already jointly branded," it added.

IVC Evidensia, which has more than 2,200 clinics, hospitals and out-of-hours site, said it welcomed the report and was focused on delivering "outstanding care for animals and excellent support for customers".

Medivet, which has more than 350 clinics across the country, said it was pleased to see that many of its existing practices already aligned with the CMA's findings.

"We further welcome the fact that the CMA's analysis has found that Medivet does not generate excess profits. We look forward to focusing on delivering exceptional care to our patients," a spokesperson added.

The British Veterinary Association, a national body that represents the veterinary profession and was not one of the six large vets named by the CMA, said the changes were a "really positive step" to increasing transparency, but it noted that vets have been dealing with higher costs in recent years.

The group's president Dr Rob Williams said: "Delivering highly skilled veterinary medicine is costly and whilst we recognise prices have risen sharply in recent years, this is due to a number of factors, including the higher costs all businesses are experiencing - and vet practices are not immune."

A list of seven locations that could be turned into new towns has been released by the government.

The areas have been put forward for consideration by ministers as part of a nationwide housebuilding programme, with each one expected to deliver at least 10,000 homes.

Once approved, the new towns will also come with schools, health facilities, green spaces and "vibrant high streets", according to the housing ministry.

National Highways said it was working with the government to ensure the towns were well connected.

Housing secretary Steve Reed said: "People want real change – homes they can afford, local infrastructure that works, and good jobs in thriving communities.

"From the ground up, we're planning whole communities with homes, jobs, transport links, and green spaces designed together — so we can give families the security and opportunities they deserve."

A public consultation on the proposed locations and draft planning policy is open until Monday 18 May - you can share your views here.

Final locations will be confirmed later this year after the consultation.

By James Sillars, business and economics reporter

While everyone has their fingers crossed for peace in the Middle East, the energy price shock rumbles on regardless.

A new forecast by the global economic advisory firm Oxford Economics suggests price spikes ahead, even if the Strait of Hormuz transit route was to reopen.

It has substantially upgraded its oil price forecasts, expecting Brent to average $114 a barrel between April and June.

The contract for May delivery stands at $102.

"We assume transit returns in May at around half of pre-conflict levels, with trade disruption easing slowly through the rest of 2026," the analysis read.

It saw natural gas costs for Europe around 61% higher.

Gas prices for UK delivery are broadly in line with those for the continent in percentage terms - up by 93% and 85%, respectively, so far this month.

"We assume some persistence to the price shock over Q2 and Q3, as European buyers replenish storage for winter", the report added.

The price levels threaten to bake in higher costs for Europe this winter.

The UK is more reliant on immediate supply, so the outlook is less clear.

While the energy price cap shields most households from any bill shocks until the beginning of July at least, a respected forecast last Friday saw an increase of more than £330 in average annual bills during the summer months - a time when bills should be at their lowest of the year.

Cornwall Insight's estimate is subject to change as markets shift violently in response to Middle East war developments.

The same can be said about the outlook for fuel, food and other essential goods. They are all either placing or set to place upwards pressure on inflation.

The price outlook will depend very much on the movement of oil and gas flows in the weeks ahead.

Council tax for the average Band D property across England and Wales will rise by 4.9% over the next year - as analysis revealed some areas would receive bills almost £500 more than others.

The Chartered Institute of Public Finance and Accountancy and Infoshare+ found the average Band D bill stands at £2,348.08.

They found that although the increase was slightly lower than last year's council tax hike of 5.1%, it remains above January's inflation rate of 3.2% - meaning households will continue to see council tax increases outpace the cost of living.

The annual council also showed the average Band D bill now exceeds £2,000 in every region, with the South West recording the highest average at £2,550.07 while Greater London remains the lowest at £2,068.28.

This means households in the South West are being forced to pay £481.79 more on average than those in the capital.

People in the South West will see the highest increase on their bills in percentage terms, at 5.4% compared with 4.4% in Greater London.

The CIPFA warned that increases alone were unlikely to fully offset rising demand for services and increasing costs with central government funding reducing in relative terms over time.

Diesel prices have hit a three-year high, latest RAC figures show.

Drivers face paying 171.17p per litre at the pump - up 29p since the end of February and the highest price since mid-January 2023.

This means a tank now costs £94 - £16 more than it did at the start of the Middle East conflict.

The average price of unleaded has also risen more than 14p a litre to 147.19p, taking the cost of filling up a family car to £81.

Petrol prices were last this high in early June 2024, it added.

RAC head of policy Simon Williams warned that prices could rise further, with this Easter on track to be "the most expensive" for fuel since the early days of the Ukraine war.

He said the price of petrol is likely to go above 150p a litre in the next week and diesel is heading to 180p.
